title: The Ropes That Were Never Snakes
story_reference: Based on story-002-the-rope-and-the-snake.md, scene: sitting by the river after the rope is revealed; the disciple recognizes his whole life as rope-snakes
verse_reference: Verses 108, 110 (the two powers of Māyā — veiling and projecting)
hook: He sat beside the river and did the math. Every panic. Every sleepless night. Every grip on control. Ropes, all of them.

script.visual: Night now. Two figures seated on a riverbank. The rope lies between them, coiled loose. The Guru does not speak. The disciple stares at the dark water.
script.voiceover: They sat by the river a long time after that. The rope lay between them. The Guru did not speak.

script.visual: Flashcuts, one second each, shot in cold blue-gray color grade: a spreadsheet opening at 2 a.m.; a fist clenched around a phone; a man in a suit, jaw tight, waiting for an elevator; an email subject line — 're: Q3 miss.' Each one lit only by screen light.
script.voiceover: The disciple thought of his corner office. The anxiety about market share. The tightness in his chest when a competitor launched a product.

script.visual: More flashcuts, same cold grade: a hand white-knuckled on a steering wheel; a voice-memo recording light, red, at 3 a.m.; a man sitting on the edge of a hotel bed in the dark.
script.voiceover: The rage when someone questioned his authority. The sleepless nights. The white-knuckled grip on control.

script.visual: Cut hard back to the riverbank. Warm firelight now. The rope. The disciple's face — still. Something behind the eyes has moved.
script.voiceover: All of it. Every pounding heart. Every panic. Every sleepless hour. Caused by snakes that were only rope.

script.visual: The Guru speaks for the first time in the reel. Unhurried. Not triumphant.
script.voiceover: The Guru said: 'Māyā has two powers. It hides what is. And it paints what is not. First it blindfolds you. Then it shows you a movie and calls it real.'

script.visual: Close on the disciple. A question comes out of him, almost against his will. His voice sounds far away, as if it is coming from someone else.
script.voiceover: 'What if everything I have believed is wrong?'

script.visual: The Guru's eyes. Clear, unflinching. A single line. Hold to black.
script.voiceover: 'Then you are finally ready to see what is right.'

cta: Scene four. The fear was real. The hands were shaking. The snake was never there. Name one rope you have been afraid of this week.
caption:
The terror was real. The sweat was real. The pounding heart was real. None of it had been caused by anything that existed.

Āvaraṇa — Māyā's first power — hides what is there.
Vikṣepa — Māyā's second power — paints what is not.

First it blindfolds you. Then it plays you a movie and calls it real.

The practice is not to stop feeling afraid. The practice is to see the rope.

āvaraṇa • vikṣepa
music_suggestion: Open on water ambience, crickets, distant night-bird. The flashcut montage is scored with a single rising synth tone — cold, anxious, building by semitone — that resolves only when we cut back to the riverbank. For the Guru's lines and the final question: silence except for the river. A single low note holds under the final frame.
casting_notes: Two characters: The Seeker (now in week-one ashram simplicity, firelight on his face — his corporate self shown only in memory cuts) and The Guru (older, speaks twice in the reel). The flashcut montage should be shot separately in a modern urban environment (office building, parking garage, hotel room) with the same actor — cold color grade, shallow focus, screen-lit. Contrast with the warm firelit riverbank is the whole point. Alternative: if the urban cuts are not feasible, replace with abstract B-roll (a spreadsheet scrolling, a phone screen filling the frame, a clock reading 3:17) — no actor required.
